title: Social Groups and Crime
examTip: For each social group (class, gender, ethnicity, age), always contrast official statistics with self-report/victim survey data — the gap between them is the analytical point examiners are looking for.
introTips: Official stats overrepresent WC, male, Black, and young people — but self-report studies complicate this.
Messerschmidt (1993): crime as 'doing gender' — masculinity explains male offending.
Stan Cohen (1972) 'Folk Devils and Moral Panics' — examiners love this for youth and ethnicity questions.
intro: If you look at prison statistics, crime in Britain looks like a story about young, working-class, Black men. But the data we have on crime is deeply shaped by who gets policed, prosecuted, and convicted — not just who commits crimes. Understanding crime by social group means interrogating the statistics themselves, not just reading them at face value.

keyPoints.heading: Social Class and Crime
keyPoints.content: Official statistics show that working-class people are significantly overrepresented in crime statistics and prison populations — 74% of prisoners left school with no qualifications. However, this picture is heavily distorted by the way crime is measured. Self-report studies show that crime is spread much more evenly across classes — middle-class crime (especially white-collar and corporate crime) is massively underreported and underpunished. White-collar crime — the term coined by Edwin Sutherland (1949) — refers to crimes committed by 'respectable' people in the course of their occupation: embezzlement, fraud, tax evasion, insider trading, false accounting. Corporate crime refers to illegal actions by businesses: health and safety violations (deaths in the workplace), illegal pollution and dumping, mis-selling scandals (the PPI scandal cost over £50 billion). These crimes cause enormous financial, environmental, and physical harm — but receive far less media attention, prosecution, and punishment than equivalent street crime. Explanations for working-class overrepresentation in official statistics: poverty → strain/relative deprivation (Merton; Left Realism); police bias (stop and search concentrated in deprived areas); harsher sentencing of working-class defendants with fewer resources to hire good lawyers.
writeBullets: Official stats — WC massively overrepresented; 74% of prisoners left school with no qualifications
Self-report studies — crime more evenly spread; middle-class crime undercounted
White-collar crime (Sutherland 1949) — crimes by respectable people in their occupation
Corporate crime — illegal actions by businesses; PPI scandal cost over £50 billion
Explanations for WC overrepresentation: poverty, police bias, harsher sentencing
Corporate crime causes enormous harm but receives less attention than street crime
keyPoints.memTip: WC crime gets headlines; MC/ruling class crime gets settlements out of court.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Sutherland (1949) is the key name for white-collar crime — ensure students know him and the date.

keyPoints.heading: Gender and Crime
keyPoints.content: Men commit approximately 80% of all recorded crime. Men are especially overrepresented in violent crime, robbery, and burglary. Women are more likely to commit: theft (particularly shoplifting) and some types of fraud. Why do men commit so much more crime? Several explanations: (a) Masculinity — James Messerschmidt (1993): crime is a resource for 'doing gender' — demonstrating masculinity when other legitimate means of masculine status (stable employment, provider role) are unavailable. Violence, toughness, and risk-taking are ways of 'accomplishing' masculinity. (b) Socialisation (Talcott Parsons): boys are socialised to be independent, risk-taking, and aggressive; girls are socialised to be conformist, nurturing, and risk-averse. (c) Social control: girls are more closely supervised by parents, have less opportunity to commit crime, and are more risk-averse. (d) Chivalry thesis (Otto Pollak, 1950): police and courts treat women more leniently — less likely to arrest, charge, convict, or impose custodial sentences. If true, this would inflate the male-to-female crime ratio in official statistics. Counter-argument: Pat Carlen (1988) — women who commit crime have often escaped abusive situations (domestic violence, exploitation); crime is a rational response to severely limited options. Prison is particularly inappropriate for women with children — separating mothers from children causes further harm.
writeBullets: Men commit approximately 80% of recorded crime — especially violence, robbery, burglary
Messerschmidt (1993) — crime as 'doing gender'; demonstrating masculinity when other means unavailable
Parsons socialisation — boys taught to be risk-taking and aggressive; girls conformist
Social control — girls more closely supervised; fewer opportunities to offend
Chivalry thesis (Pollak 1950) — women treated more leniently by police and courts
Pat Carlen (1988) — women's crime often rational response to abuse and limited options
keyPoints.memTip: Men + crime = masculinity theory (Messerschmidt). Women + leniency = chivalry thesis (Pollak).
keyPoints.tutor_note: The chivalry thesis is heavily contested — some research shows women are actually treated MORE harshly (especially if they deviate from feminine norms, e.g., violent women). This is a strong evaluation point.

keyPoints.heading: Ethnicity and Crime
keyPoints.content: Official statistics show that Black Caribbean men are significantly overrepresented in stop and search, arrest, prosecution, and imprisonment. In 2022 (Home Office data): Black people were stopped and searched at 7 times the rate of white people. Black people are approximately 3 times more likely to be arrested than white people. However, self-report studies show much smaller differences in actual offending rates between ethnic groups — suggesting that the gap in official statistics reflects differential policing and criminal justice processing rather than differential behaviour. The concept of institutional racism is central here. The Macpherson Report (1999) — produced after the racist murder of Stephen Lawrence and the Metropolitan Police's failure to properly investigate it — defined institutional racism as 'the collective failure of an organisation to provide an appropriate and professional service to people because of their colour, culture or ethnic origin — through unwitting prejudice, ignorance, thoughtlessness, and racist stereotyping.' This applies to policing directly. From the Left Realist perspective (Lea and Young, 1984), elevated crime rates in some Black communities reflect relative deprivation and marginalisation — structural causes, not cultural pathology. Stuart Hall (1978) — the 'mugging' moral panic deliberately scapegoated Black youth to deflect from economic crisis.
writeBullets: Black people stopped and searched at 7× the rate of white people (Home Office 2022)
Self-report studies show smaller ethnic differences in offending than official stats suggest
Institutional racism (Macpherson Report 1999) — collective failure to serve people due to race
Stephen Lawrence — murdered 1993; Metropolitan Police failed to investigate properly
Left Realism (Lea and Young 1984) — elevated crime explained by relative deprivation and marginalisation
Stuart Hall (1978) — moral panic about Black muggers used to distract from economic crisis
keyPoints.memTip: Ethnic overrepresentation in crime stats = policing choices + institutional racism, not just behaviour.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The Macpherson Report (1999) is a landmark — ensure students know what it was responding to (Stephen Lawrence murder) and its key definition of institutional racism.

keyPoints.heading: Age and Crime
keyPoints.content: Crime peaks in the mid-to-late teenage years — the 15–24 age group commits a disproportionate amount of recorded crime — and declines steadily with age. Youth crime concerns include: street crime, gang involvement, anti-social behaviour. Moral panics about youth crime repeatedly exaggerate the threat. Stan Cohen (1972) — 'Folk Devils and Moral Panics': the concept originated in his study of the Mods and Rockers disturbances at Clacton on Easter bank holiday 1964. A relatively minor incident (some fighting, small-scale vandalism) was transformed by media coverage into a major national crisis — with exaggerated reports of violence, warnings of an imminent repeat, and demands for police crackdowns. This illustrates labelling theory applied at the societal level: the media and authorities label a group as 'folk devils' → public moral outrage → heightened policing → more arrests → statistics 'confirm' the original panic. Youth subcultures: Albert Cohen (1955, no relation to Stan) — 'status frustration': working-class boys who fail in school seek status through delinquent subcultures that invert mainstream values. Chicago School — delinquency thrives in socially disorganised urban areas. At the other end of the age spectrum: older people are significantly underrepresented in crime statistics for most crime types, especially violent crime. However, older, more powerful people may commit disproportionate white-collar and corporate crime that is rarely recorded in statistics.
writeBullets: Crime peaks in 15–24 age group and declines with age
Stan Cohen (1972) 'Folk Devils and Moral Panics' — Mods and Rockers at Clacton 1964
Moral panic — media exaggerates youth deviance; labelling at societal level
Folk devils — groups constructed by media as symbolic threats to social order
Albert Cohen (1955) status frustration — WC boys fail in school; seek status through delinquent subculture
Older people underrepresented in crime stats but may commit more white-collar crime
keyPoints.memTip: Two Cohens: STAN Cohen = moral panics; ALBERT Cohen = status frustration. Don't mix them up.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The two Cohens are a classic student confusion point — drill the distinction: Stan = moral panics (1972); Albert = status frustration (1955).

Crime more evenly spread — MC white-collar crime massively undercounted
Poverty and strain (Merton); police bias; harsher sentencing; white-collar crime ignored (Sutherland 1949)
Commit ~80% of recorded crime; especially violence, robbery
Gender gap slightly smaller in self-report but still significant
Doing gender (Messerschmidt 1993); socialisation (Parsons); less social control; chivalry thesis (Pollak 1950)

Self-report shows smaller ethnic differences in offending
Institutional racism (Macpherson 1999); differential policing; relative deprivation and marginalisation (Lea and Young 1984)
Peak offending age group; overrepresented in street crime, gang crime
Youth crime may be exaggerated by moral panics (Stan Cohen 1972)
Status frustration (Albert Cohen 1955); moral panics; socialisation into risk-taking; boredom and marginalisation

0.question: James Messerschmidt (1993) argues that young men commit more crime than women because of which factor?
options: Crime is a way of 'doing gender' — demonstrating masculinity when other means of status are unavailable
Men have lower intelligence and less ability to assess risks
Women are treated more harshly by the courts and are less likely to reoffend
Men face more relative deprivation than women, making crime economically rational
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Messerschmidt argued that crime — especially violence — is one way young men 'accomplish' masculinity when legitimate routes (stable job, respected status) are blocked by class or structural disadvantage.
0.wrongFeedback: Messerschmidt's theory is specifically about gender identity — about what it means to be masculine in a society that values strength, dominance, and risk-taking. Which option relates to that idea?

2.question: The Macpherson Report (1999) was published in response to which event, and what key concept did it introduce?
options: The racist murder of Stephen Lawrence; it introduced the concept of institutional racism
The Mods and Rockers disturbances at Clacton 1964; it introduced the concept of moral panic
The PPI mis-selling scandal; it introduced the concept of corporate crime
The discovery of the dark figure of crime; it introduced the Crime Survey for England and Wales
2.correctFeedback: Correct. The Macpherson Report was a public inquiry into the Metropolitan Police's failed investigation of Stephen Lawrence's murder in 1993. It concluded that the police were institutionally racist — that collective failures, not just individual prejudice, produced discrimination.
2.wrongFeedback: Think about the late 1990s and a high-profile case of racial injustice and police failure. The Macpherson Report was a watershed moment for race relations and policing in the UK.

commonMistakes: Saying ethnic minorities 'commit more crime' without qualification — official statistics reflect policing choices and institutional racism, not just behaviour. Always reference the self-report evidence.
Confusing Stan Cohen (1972, moral panics) with Albert Cohen (1955, status frustration) — two different sociologists, two different theories.
Saying the chivalry thesis explains all gender differences in crime — many researchers dispute it; some argue women are treated more harshly when their crime violates feminine norms.
Forgetting white-collar crime when discussing class and crime — the point about MC crime being undercounted is crucial and often missed.
Treating Mods and Rockers as evidence of real widespread violence — Stan Cohen's argument was that the media's panic was disproportionate to the actual events.
examTips: For any 'explain patterns of crime' question, structure your answer around: official statistics → self-report evidence → sociological explanation. Three steps, each worth marks.
The Macpherson Report (1999) and the concept of institutional racism can be applied to ethnicity and crime, policing, and social control questions.
When evaluating chivalry thesis answers, note that it is disputed — some research shows women face harsher sentences when they deviate from gender norms.
revisionCardBullets: Official stats — WC, male, young, Black overrepresented; but statistics reflect policing choices
White-collar crime (Sutherland 1949) — crimes by respectable people in their occupation; massively undercounted
Corporate crime — illegal actions by businesses; PPI scandal, health and safety violations
Men commit ~80% of recorded crime — especially violent and property crime
Messerschmidt (1993) — crime as 'doing gender'; accomplishing masculinity
Chivalry thesis (Pollak 1950) — women treated more leniently by police and courts
Pat Carlen (1988) — women's crime often rational response to abuse and limited options
Black people stopped at 7× rate of white people (Home Office 2022)
Institutional racism (Macpherson Report 1999) — collective organisational failure based on race
Stephen Lawrence murder (1993) — triggered Macpherson inquiry
Stan Cohen (1972) 'Folk Devils and Moral Panics' — Mods and Rockers at Clacton 1964
Albert Cohen (1955) status frustration — WC boys fail in school; seek status through delinquent subculture
